reasoning: FALSE POSITIVE. The core claim — that an attacker can pass context=product-ids or context=product-ids-ff via the HTTP request to trigger the product-ID enumeration branch — is wrong. The $context parameter in getSearchResults($phrase='', $return=false, $context='autocomplete') is a PHP function parameter with a hardcoded default of 'autocomplete'. It is never populated from $_REQUEST, $_GET, or $_POST anywhere in the function or its callers. When WordPress fires the method via wp_ajax_nopriv_ or wc_ajax_ with zero arguments, $context is always 'autocomplete', making the product-ids branch (line 263) and product-ids-ff branch (line 279) completely unreachable via HTTP. The only callers that pass 'product-ids' as $context are internal PHP callers: Helpers::searchProducts() (Helpers.php:1660) and Search::doSearch() (includes/Search.php:67), both of which pass $return=true so results go back to PHP, not echoed as AJAX JSON. A grep of the entire plugin source confirms $context is never read from $_REQUEST — the only REQUEST['context'] usage in the codebase is in Blocks.php for a REST API edit-context check, unrelated to search. The branches exist in the code but are not reachable by an unauthenticated HTTP caller supplying any request parameter.
